title: Measure before optimization

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific React: Component-Based Browser Applications draft for Measure before optimization; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: React Learn and Reference; React 19.2; provisions applicable to Measure before optimization
sources.claim: React performance work starts with measured user impact, component render cost, network, bundle, and DOM evidence.

sources.locator: React Versions; 19.2.7, June 2026; provisions applicable to Measure before optimization
sources.claim: React performance work starts with measured user impact, component render cost, network, bundle, and DOM evidence.

canonicalExplanation.summary: React performance work starts with measured user impact, component render cost, network, bundle, and DOM evidence.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Profiler evidence identifies a slow 5000-row render before pagination or virtualization is selected. Your goal is to record a render-performance observation and justify either a targeted optimization or no change. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Routing and application context; Remote data and asynchronous UI; React developer tools; Minimal and normalized state. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: React performance work starts with measured user impact, component render cost, network, bundle, and DOM evidence. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Development timings differ, rerenders can be cheap, virtualization affects accessibility, and premature caching adds complexity. This draft therefore rejects: Every rerender is a performance bug that should be prevented.

includes: Use React performance tools to identify actual expensive renders before memoizing.
Record a render-performance observation and justify either a targeted optimization or no change.
excludes: Development timings differ, rerenders can be cheap, virtualization affects accessibility, and premature caching adds complexity.

outcomes: Record a render-performance observation and justify either a targeted optimization or no change.

examples.title: Measure before optimization: concrete case
examples.situation: Profiler evidence identifies a slow 5000-row render before pagination or virtualization is selected.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: Profiler evidence identifies a slow 5000-row render before pagination or virtualization is selected.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Rerenders are normal; optimize measured expensive work and verify the user-visible improvement.
examples.result: The result follows because Rerenders are normal; optimize measured expensive work and verify the user-visible improvement.

examples.title: Measure before optimization: boundary case
examples.situation: Development timings differ, rerenders can be cheap, virtualization affects accessibility, and premature caching adds complexity.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: Development timings differ, rerenders can be cheap, virtualization affects accessibility, and premature caching adds complexity.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: Every rerender is a performance bug that should be prevented.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Rerenders are normal; optimize measured expensive work and verify the user-visible improvement.

misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use a concrete React: Component-Based Browser Applications artifact to explain why this belief fails: Every rerender is a performance bug that should be prevented.

analogy.example: A state-driven component workshop can model Measure before optimization, where inputs and state produce a UI description.
usefulFor: Building a first mental model for Measure before optimization before using exact platform terms
limitations: A workshop does not reproduce reconciliation identity, hook ordering, concurrent rendering, effects, or browser accessibility APIs.

recap.summary: You should now be able to record a render-performance observation and justify either a targeted optimization or no change. Keep this boundary in view: Development timings differ, rerenders can be cheap, virtualization affects accessibility, and premature caching adds complexity.
retrievalPrompts: Explain Measure before optimization using exact React: Component-Based Browser Applications terms.
Why is this claim incorrect: Every rerender is a performance bug that should be prevented.
Apply checkpoint 'measured expensive work' to a new artifact.
recap.nextReviewPrompt: Next: continue to “Memoization and stable identity”. Before moving on, answer one retrieval prompt without reopening the explanation.
